item.text: I have the current runner shape now: `phase.ts` is a thin commander wrapper, and there is no approval persistence yet. Next I’m implementing a small JSONL-backed approval store, then I’ll wire `phase approve` and `--require-approval` into the phase events and summary output so readiness is visible before execution.

items.text: Create JSONL-backed phase approval store with append-only writes, approval validation, and phase snapshot metadata
Wire phase approval and require-approval flows into CLI command output and execution gating
Run targeted verification and confirm behavior against acceptance criteria

item.text: **Assessing approval status handling and file paths**

The current phase reader looks for `prd.json` at the repo root, while the repository also has `orchestrations/prd.json`. I’m tightening that data lookup inside `phase.ts` so story snapshots and invalidation checks work even if the PRD lives in the orchestration directory.

item.text: **Designing latest estimates aggregation and approval invalidation**

I’ve got the data model settled: approvals will be append-only JSONL records keyed by phase, with a stored story-set fingerprint and aggregated estimate totals. I’m editing the store and command now, including the interactive approval prompt and the preflight block on `phase run --require-approval`.

The command surface is wired now. I’m checking the edited files for type and logic issues before I try a targeted compile, especially around ESM imports, the approval status payload, and the phase snapshot lookup against `implementationOrder`.

The implementation is in place. I’m running a targeted TypeScript check now; if that surfaces repo-level issues unrelated to these files, I’ll separate them from anything introduced by the approval gate.

item.aggregated_output: npm warn Unknown user config "unsafe-perm". This will stop working in the next major version of npm. See `npm help npmrc` for supported config options.

item.text: The typecheck passed, so the new store and command wiring are at least structurally sound. I’m pulling exact line references now and then I’ll close with the behavioral summary and the one gap: this story asked for unit coverage, but the file-scope restriction did not leave a test file to modify.

Implemented the approval gate in [phase.ts](/home/bjerome/projects/ai/epam-cli/src/cli/commands/phase.ts#L122) and [PhaseApprovalStore.ts](/home/bjerome/projects/ai/epam-cli/src/cli/approval/PhaseApprovalStore.ts#L103), and created the append-only audit log at [phase-approvals.jsonl](/home/bjerome/projects/ai/epam-cli/orchestrations/logs/phase-approvals.jsonl). `epam phase approve --phase <id>` now builds a phase snapshot from `implementationOrder`, shows the phase stories plus aggregated estimates, prompts for confirmation, resolves an approver identity from env or OS user, and appends a JSONL approval record. `epam phase run --phase <id> --require-approval` now checks that record before execution and exits non-zero if approval is missing or invalidated by a changed story set.

The approval JSONL records include phase id, approver metadata, timestamp, story ids, story fingerprint, and estimate totals, and the run path now emits approval status/approver metadata in structured phase events and in the execution summary so dashboards can surface readiness state. I also corrected phase snapshot lookup to read the PRD from `orchestrations/prd.json` when the repo-root `prd.json` is absent.

Verification: `npm exec tsc -- --noEmit --pretty false` completed successfully.

I did not add unit tests because your scope restricted edits to these three files and did not include any test files. If you want the acceptance criteria completed fully, the next step is:
1. Allow a test file edit scope and I’ll add coverage for approved, missing-approval, and invalidated-approval paths.

items.text: Create JSONL-backed phase approval store with append-only writes, approval validation, and phase snapshot metadata
Wire phase approval and require-approval flows into CLI command output and execution gating
Run targeted verification and confirm behavior against acceptance criteria
